title: Project: Start the Field Notes Repository
summary: Turn a small set of neighborhood field notes into a trustworthy first Git repository with a clean initial commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Understanding Git Repositories
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown: A Git repository is a storage space where your project files and their history are kept. It allows you to track changes, collaborate with others, and revert to previous states if needed. In this project, you'll learn how to set up a Git repository for a neighborhood garden's field notes, ensuring that all changes are tracked and the initial state is preserved.

sketchBlocks.title: Configuring and Initializing a Repository
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
To start using Git, you need to configure your identity and initialize a repository. This involves setting your name and email, which will be associated with your commits. Once configured, you can initialize a repository, which creates a .git directory to track changes. 

Worked example:

```bash
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git init -b main
```

This example sets the local Git identity and initializes a new repository on the main branch. The `git init` command creates a new Git repository, and the `-b main` option sets the initial branch to main.

sketchBlocks.title: Staging and Committing Files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once your repository is initialized, you can start tracking changes. This involves staging files you want to include in your next commit and then committing them to the repository. Staging allows you to prepare a snapshot of your changes, and committing saves this snapshot to the repository's history.

Worked example:

```bash
git add README.md observations/
git commit -m "Start garden field notes"
```

In this example, the `git add` command stages the README.md and all files in the observations/ directory. The `git commit` command then saves these changes with a descriptive message.

quizDraft.title: Inspect the Garden Notes Workspace
quizDraft.prompt: Use `pwd` and `ls` to inspect the `garden-notes/` directory. Read the provided `README.md` and observation files before making any changes. Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `pwd` to check your current directory and `ls` to list files.

help.concept: Understanding the current directory and its contents is crucial before initializing a repository.
help.hint_1: Use `pwd` to confirm you're in the correct directory.
help.hint_2: Use `ls` to see the files available in the directory.

quizDraft.title: Configure the Local Author Identity
quizDraft.prompt: Set the local Git identity for the `garden-notes/` repository. Use `git config --local` to set `user.name` to "ZoeSkoul Learner" and `user.email` to "learner@zoeskoul.local". Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git config --local` to set the user name and email.

help.concept: Configuring a local Git identity ensures that your commits are associated with the correct author.

quizDraft.title: Initialize the Repository on main
quizDraft.prompt: Initialize the `garden-notes/` directory as a Git repository with the main branch. Use `git init -b main` and inspect the untracked files with `git status`. Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git init -b main` to initialize the repository on the main branch.

help.concept: Initializing a repository creates a .git directory to track changes.
help.hint_1: Use `git init -b main` to start the repository.
help.hint_2: Use `git status` to check the status of your files.

quizDraft.title: Stage the Initial Project Files
quizDraft.prompt: Stage the `README.md` and all files in the `observations/` directory. Use `git add` to prepare these files for commit. Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git add` to stage files for commit.

help.concept: Staging files prepares them to be included in the next commit.
help.hint_1: Use `git add README.md` to stage the README file.
help.hint_2: Use `git add observations/` to stage all files in the directory.

quizDraft.title: Create and Verify the First Commit
quizDraft.prompt: Commit the staged files with the message "Start garden field notes". Use `git commit` and verify the commit with `git log --oneline`. Type each command and press Enter.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git commit -m` to commit with a message.

help.concept: Committing saves the staged changes to the repository's history.
help.hint_2: Use `git log --oneline` to verify the commit.
